title: How to Use Intuition When Investing Money
meta_description: Trust your gut with money. This article shows you how to use intuition for smarter investing, beyond spreadsheets and logic.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Investing Money
og_description: Trust your gut with money. This article shows you how to use intuition for smarter investing, beyond spreadsheets and logic.
body:
You Already Know More Than You Think About Money

Let me guess. You've made financial decisions that felt "right" in your gut, and they worked out. And you've made decisions that looked perfect on paper, ticked every logical box, and they blew up in your face. You're not alone. That's not bad luck. That's your intuition trying to get your attention while your brain is busy running spreadsheets.

Here's the thing nobody tells you about money: it's not just numbers. Money carries energy. It carries stories, fears, hopes, and patterns that run way deeper than any quarterly report ever could. And if you're someone who feels things intensely - who walks into a room and knows the mood before anyone speaks - you've got a built-in financial radar that most people spend their whole lives trying to develop.

But here's the problem. You've been told your whole life that feelings don't belong in investing. That intuition is for choosing a partner or picking a restaurant, not for deciding where to put your retirement savings. That's a lie. A well-meaning lie, maybe, but a lie all the same. The most successful investors I know don't just crunch numbers. They listen to something deeper. And so can you.

So let's get real about how to use intuition when investing money - without losing your shirt, without ignoring the facts, and without feeling like you're making it all up as you go along.

Why Your Intuition Is Actually a Financial Superpower

First, let's clear something up. Intuition isn't magic. It's not psychic fortune-telling where you just "know" which stock will triple next week. That's wishful thinking dressed up in spiritual clothes, and it'll get you into trouble every time.

Real intuition is your nervous system processing information that your conscious brain hasn't caught up with yet. It's pattern recognition running at lightning speed. It's your body telling you, "Something's off here," before your mind can articulate why. And when it comes to money, that's incredibly valuable - because markets are full of information that doesn't show up on any balance sheet.

"Your gut feeling about money isn't there to replace the numbers. It's there to tell you which numbers actually matter. The spreadsheet shows you price. Your intuition shows you value."

Think about it. You've walked into a house and known within thirty seconds whether it could be your home. You've met someone and known within five minutes whether you could trust them. That same capacity works with investments. You just have to learn how to listen to it without letting fear or greed drown it out.

Does that land? Good. Because this is where most people get stuck. They think intuition means "do whatever feels good in the moment." That's not intuition - that's impulse. Intuition is calm. It's steady. It doesn't shout. It whispers. And it usually says the same thing your gut says when you're about to make a mistake: "Wait. Look closer. Something's not right here."

The Two Biggest Lies About Intuition and Investing

Lie #1: Intuition and logic are opposites.

They're not. They're partners. Logic gives you the map. Intuition tells you which roads are actually safe to travel. The best investors use both. They do the research, run the numbers, look at the fundamentals. And then they sit quietly and ask themselves, "How does this feel?" If the numbers look great but something feels wrong, they dig deeper. If the numbers look shaky but something feels right, they investigate why.

You don't have to choose between being analytical and being intuitive. You get to be both. That's your birthright as a human being.

Lie #2: You can't trust your gut with money because money is "serious."

Who decided that? Money is serious, sure. But so is your health. So are your relationships. So is your sanity. And you use intuition in all of those areas all the time. You wouldn't marry someone just because their credit score was good. Why would you invest in something just because the P/E ratio looked attractive?

Money touches every part of your life. It's connected to your sense of safety, your freedom, your ability to care for the people you love. Of course your intuition has something to say about it. The question is whether you're willing to listen.

How to Actually Do This: A Step-by-Step Process

Okay, so you're on board. You want to use intuition in your investing. But how? Let me give you a process that works. It's not complicated, but it takes practice. Fair warning: it'll feel awkward at first. That's normal. Stick with it.

Step 1: Do Your Homework First

Intuition doesn't work in a vacuum. You can't intuit your way through something you know nothing about. So start with the basics. Learn what the company does. Read the financial statements. Understand the industry. Look at the competition. Get clear on the numbers.

This isn't optional. It's the foundation. Your intuition needs something to work with. It's like a chef who needs ingredients before they can cook. The better your ingredients, the better the meal. The better your research, the more reliable your intuition becomes.

Here's a resource that can help you get grounded in the fundamentals without getting lost in jargon: See on Amazon (paid link). It's a straightforward guide to understanding what you're actually looking at when you see a stock ticker or a balance sheet. No fluff, just what you need to know.

Step 2: Create Space to Feel

After you've done the research, step away from the screen. Don't make a decision immediately. Give yourself at least 24 hours, sometimes longer. During that time, don't look at the numbers. Don't read more articles. Don't ask for opinions. Just let the information settle.

This is where most people mess up. They do the research, then they immediately try to decide. That's like cooking a meal and eating it while it's still raw. You need time for the information to digest. Your brain needs to process what it's learned. Your body needs to register how it feels.

During this waiting period, pay attention to your physical sensations. Do you feel tightness in your chest when you think about this investment? That's not random. That's information. Do you feel a sense of expansion, like something is opening up? That's also information. Neither one is automatically right or wrong - but both are worth noticing.

Step 3: Ask the Right Questions

When you're ready to check in with your intuition, don't ask yes/no questions. Those are too easy to manipulate. Instead, ask open-ended questions that give your intuition room to speak. Try these:

  • "What does this investment want me to know that I'm not seeing?"
  • "How would I feel if I had to hold this for five years?"
  • "What's the story I'm telling myself about this opportunity?"
  • "If I had no fear about money, would I still want to do this?"
  • "What am I hoping will happen that I haven't admitted to myself?"

Sit with each question for a few minutes. Don't force an answer. Just let whatever comes up come up. You might get words, images, physical sensations, or just a sense of knowing. Trust whatever shows up. It doesn't have to make logical sense right now.

Step 4: Cross-Check With Your Body

Here's a technique I teach all my students. Imagine you've already made the investment. Really imagine it. See the confirmation screen. Feel what it's like to have that money committed. Now check your body.

If you feel a sense of relief, like "finally, I've decided," that's usually a green light. If you feel a knot in your stomach, a tightness in your throat, or a vague sense of dread, that's a yellow light at minimum. If you feel nothing at all - numb, disconnected, blank - that's usually a sign that you're not connected to your intuition right now. Give it more time.

This body check is your most reliable tool. Your brain can rationalize anything. Your body tells the truth. It doesn't care about making money or looking smart. It just tells you what it knows. And what it knows is worth listening to.

When Intuition Looks Like Fear (And When It's Really Wisdom)

This is the trickiest part. How do you know if your gut feeling is intuition or just fear? Because let's be honest - investing is scary. Markets go up and down. You can lose money. Fear is a natural response.

Here's how I tell the difference. Fear feels tight, constricted, and urgent. It says things like "hurry up and sell" or "don't you dare buy" in a panicked voice. Intuition feels calm, clear, and patient. It says things like "this doesn't feel aligned" or "wait for more information" in a steady voice.

Fear wants you to act now. Intuition is fine waiting. Fear is reactive. Intuition is responsive. If you feel pressured, that's fear. If you feel a quiet certainty that doesn't need to prove anything, that's intuition.

One way to test this: ask yourself, "If I had all the time in the world and no pressure, would I still feel this way?" If the answer is yes, it's probably intuition. If the answer is "well, I need to decide before the market opens," it's probably fear.

Right?! That distinction alone has saved me more money than any stock tip I've ever gotten.

Common Mistakes People Make When They Start Using Intuition

You're going to mess this up sometimes. That's fine. Here are the most common mistakes so you can avoid the worst of them.

Mistake #1: Using intuition as an excuse to skip research. I said this above, but it's worth repeating. Intuition is not a shortcut. It's a complement to good research, not a replacement for it. If you're skipping the numbers because you "just have a feeling," you're not using intuition. You're being lazy. Don't do that.

Mistake #2: Confusing excitement with intuition. Excitement feels like energy, butterflies, and a sense of urgency. That's not intuition. That's dopamine. Real intuition about an investment usually feels more like a quiet nod of recognition. It's not flashy. It's not dramatic. It's just... right.

Mistake #3: Ignoring your intuition when it says no. This one hurts. You've done the research. The numbers look good. Everyone else is buying. But something feels off. And you buy anyway, because you don't want to miss out. Then you lose money. And you kick yourself because you knew. You knew.

Here's the thing: your intuition doesn't have to be right every time to be worth listening to. It just has to be right more often than your fear. And it will be, if you practice. Every time you ignore it and regret it, that's data. Every time you listen and it works out, that's also data. Over time, you build trust.

If you want to go deeper into how to distinguish intuition from other mental noise, this book is one of the clearest I've found: See on Amazon (paid link). It gives you a practical approach for making decisions that feel aligned, not just logical.

Building Your Intuition Muscle Over Time

Like any skill, using intuition for investing gets easier with practice. Here's how to train yourself.

Start small. Don't try to intuit your way through a major life-changing investment on your first try. Practice on small decisions. Should you buy that stock you've been watching? Should you hold or sell a small position? Should you invest in that index fund or a different one? The stakes are lower, so the pressure is lower, which means your intuition can speak more clearly.

Keep a journal. Write down every investment decision you make, what your intuition said, what your logic said, and what happened. Over time, you'll start to see patterns. You'll notice that your intuition was right about certain types of situations and wrong about others. You'll learn when to trust it and when to dig deeper.

Meditate regularly. I know, I know - everyone says that. But here's why it matters for investing specifically. Meditation trains you to notice your thoughts without being controlled by them. That's exactly what you need when the market is tanking and your fear is screaming at you to sell everything. Meditation helps you hear your intuition underneath all that noise.

Practice on non-financial decisions too. Use your intuition to choose what to eat, which route to drive, who to call. The more you use it, the stronger it gets. And the stronger it gets, the more you'll trust it when real money is on the line.

The Deeper Truth: Money Is a Mirror

Here's the part nobody talks about. Your relationship with money is a direct reflection of your relationship with yourself. If you don't trust yourself, you won't trust your financial decisions. If you're afraid of your own power, you'll make small, safe investments that never grow. If you believe you don't deserve abundance, you'll find ways to lose money even when you have it.

Using intuition for investing isn't just about making better financial decisions. It's about learning to trust yourself at a fundamental level. Every time you listen to your gut and it works out, you're building a deeper sense of self-trust. Every time you ignore it and regret it, you're learning what happens when you abandon yourself.

This is why I call it "The Bright Wound." The same sensitivity that makes you feel everything so intensely - the same sensitivity that's been called "too much" your whole life - is exactly what you need to work through money with wisdom. Your sensitivity isn't a weakness. It's your superpower. You just have to learn how to use it.

And that takes practice. It takes patience. It takes a willingness to make mistakes and learn from them. But it's worth it. Because when you learn to trust your intuition with money, you're not just building wealth. You're building a relationship with yourself that can't be shaken by market fluctuations or bad quarters or any external circumstance.

Know what I mean? You're building a foundation that lasts.

If you're ready to take this deeper - to understand not just how to use intuition in investing, but how to rebuild your entire relationship with money from the inside out - this book walks you through the whole process: See on Amazon (paid link). It's not about getting rich quick. It's about getting clear on what you actually want, and then using your intuition to get there.

Final Thoughts: Start Where You Are

You don't need to be a psychic to use intuition for investing. You don't need to meditate for hours or have some special gift. You just need to be willing to listen to what you already know, underneath all the noise and fear and shoulds.

Start with one small decision this week. Pick an investment you're considering - even if it's just a few dollars. Do the research. Then sit quietly and feel into it. Ask yourself the questions I gave you. Check your body. See what comes up.

You might be surprised at how much you already know. And you might be surprised at how good it feels to trust yourself with money. That's the real investment - in your own sovereignty, your own wisdom, your own ability to work through this wild world with both your head and your heart fully engaged.

You've got this. You've always had this. You just needed permission to use it.
